sources.notes: Read the complete statement and constraints, including maximizing B-A, minimizing A among ties, t <= 1000, and l,r,G <= 1e18.

Read the official editorial section and embedded C++ solution. The tutorial prose widget was unavailable, but the code confirms rounding to endpoint multiples of G and enumerating total endpoint shrink before testing gcd.

Actually read. It independently derives the scaled coprime-pair problem and proves a constant endpoint-search bound: one candidate among the first 6 scaled integers has only prime factors at least 7, and one of the last 17 is coprime to it.

Re-derived the objective and tie-break encoded by the nested-loop order, proved the singleton scaled value 1 case, and checked the algorithm against exhaustive pair search on 123240 small (l,r,G) cases.

annotation.summary: Choose l <= A <= B <= r with gcd(A,B)=G, maximizing B-A and then minimizing A, or report that no pair exists.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 1000 and 1 <= l <= r,G <= 1e18. Iteration proportional to r-l is impossible; arithmetic and gcd calls must use 64-bit integers.
annotation.core_idea: Let L=ceil(l/G) and R=floor(r/G). Every feasible pair has A=G*x and B=G*y with L<=x<=y<=R, and gcd(A,B)=G exactly when gcd(x,y)=1. If L>R there is no pair. If L=R, the pair is valid only for L=R=1, yielding A=B=G. Otherwise enumerate total endpoint shrink s=0,1,... and, for j=0..s, test x=L+j and y=R-(s-j). Every pair in that layer has distance G*(R-L-s), so the first successful layer maximizes distance; increasing j makes the first success in that layer minimize A. This brute force is bounded: for a long interval, among L..L+5 choose a number not divisible by 2,3,5. It has at most 12 distinct prime factors, all at least 7, below 1e18. In any 17 consecutive integers, the union bound sum ceil(17/p) over those factors is at most 16, so some y in R-16..R is coprime to it. Thus a solution is found after at most 21 total endpoint removals; short intervals are already of constant size.
annotation.complexity: O(C^2 log U) time and O(1) memory per test, where C=21 and U<=1e18; at most 253 gcd tests are needed.
tricks: Divide out the required gcd and round the original interval to valid multiples.
Enumerate total endpoint loss so search order exactly matches maximum distance.
Enumerate left loss second so the first pair also has the minimum A tie-break.
Use a prime-factor coverage bound to make the apparently unbounded brute force constant.
Treat the lone scaled value 1 as the only valid equal-endpoint case.

solution_variants.name: scaled-coprime-endpoint-enumeration
solution_variants.summary: Scale the interval by G, then test pairs in nondecreasing total distance from the two outer endpoints and increasing left endpoint until finding a coprime pair.
solution_variants.complexity: O(C^2 log U) time and O(1) memory with C=21 and U<=1e18

tags.evidence: Dividing A and B by G converts gcd(A,B)=G into the exact coprimality condition gcd(A/G,B/G)=1, which is both the reduction and the acceptance test.

The intended algorithm explicitly enumerates endpoint-shrink layers and candidate pairs; the number-theoretic endpoint bound proves this exhaustive search performs only a constant number of gcd tests.
Feasible original values must be multiples of G, so ceiling and floor division produce the exact scaled interval before coprimality is tested.
